metadata.title: Piloting text-mining in a mixed-method evaluation on human rights: Management propositions for small public administrations

abstract: Text-mining was piloted in an evaluation of the Human Rights-Based Approach in Finland's development cooperation. Textual and numerical evidence was extracted from project documents using rules-based, machine learning and topic modelling techniques. This practice-oriented article exemplifies considerations for evaluation managers from a civil servant's perspective. It discusses obstacles to engaging with these techniques identified by literature. It demonstrates the feasibility of piloting new technologies for public administrations with limited human resources, emphasising guidance from the evaluation manager, staff training and early collaboration with data scientists and partners. Text-mining offers new ways of generating and analysing data. The limitations call for mixed-methods, evaluative judgement by evaluators and human oversight by the evaluation manager. Although resource-intensive, employing these methods does not require one to become a data scientist but to understand what kind of evidence can be generated. Management propositions are provided.

paragraphs: Public administrations have been digitalising for a long time. Evolving from 'e-governance', data science methodologies and artificial intelligence (DSAI) represent the latest generation of data-driven technologies (Dunleavy and Margetts, 2023). This article is concerned with DSAI and new technologies in general, inclusive of data science techniques such as rules-based analysis, big data, machine learning, artificial intelligence and others (see Bamberger and Mabry, 2020;Dunleavy and Margetts, 2023;Hossin et al., 2023).

DSAI technologies within public administrations are themselves developing. They can offer insights for customised policies or services and provide answers to a broad range of managerial questions (Beland and Sadiku, 2018;Hautamäki and Oksanen, 2018;Hossin et al., 2023;Kowalski et al., 2020;Schildt, 2017). Digital tools are not new to evaluation. They have improved the quality of products, increased timeliness and cost-efficiencies, reduced errors and assisted in quantitative data analysis and project management (Henley et al., 2023;Jamieson and Azzam, 2012). National government agencies are some of the largest procurers of evaluations. They can influence the demand for new technologies (Nielsen, 2023).

The interest in unstructured textual data dates to the 1950s with the first digitised texts. Yet, machine learning is relatively recent (DEval German Institute of Development Evaluation, 2019). Artificial intelligence is now available for the masses and being tested for evaluation purposes (Raimondo et al., 2023).

Many have explored DSAI methods in development evaluations (Almås et al., 2018;DEval German Institute of Development Evaluation, 2019;Engström and Norén, 2021;Garcia, 2021;Howlett, 2019;Jasper et al., 2019;Laureate et al., 2023;Polak et al., 2021;World Bank, 2020), even though evaluators have been somewhat slow in taking on these new technologies (Nielsen, 2023;Picciotto, 2019).

Using natural language processing in policy research is a recent example of a multidisciplinary convergence (Hossin et al., 2023). However, collaboration between data scientists, evaluators and researchers is not automatic. Some of the challenging areas include assessing unintended outcomes; assessing data quality; construct validity; sources of bias; approaches to attribution, causality and prediction; the role of theory; understanding the difference between monitoring and evaluation; the importance of the human dimension as well as ethical and political concerns (Bamberger and Mabry, 2020;Bamberger, 2017;Frontier Technologies et al., 2020;UN Global Pulse, 2016;World Bank, 2020).

Despite progress in the public sector, many constraints remain (Hossin et al., 2023;Reutter, 2022;Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). This article links the literature on obstacles to adopting DSAI and the solutions applied in the pilot case evaluation. The article contributes to addressing the research gap in how new technologies are used in public administration and what are the sector-specific obstacles and lessons (Hossin et al., 2023;Neumann et al., 2022;Nielsen, 2023;Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). It adds knowledge on management implications for public offices that consider using text-mining as part of their evaluations in development policy and cooperation.

The role of the evaluation manager has sometimes been overlooked in introducing digital to evaluation teams (UN Global Pulse, 2016). Recognition of the role of civil servants in mediating the use of new technologies is needed (Plantinga, 2024). This article illustrates the role of the evaluation manager who is not a data scientist but a public servant and a commissioner of evaluations. The article contributes to the evaluation community by illustrating various considerations from the evaluation manager's perspective, less commonly represented in the literature. Organisations using similar typologies, for instance, the gender sensitive-transformative scale, may benefit from this article. This article does not go into the full technical depths of the evaluation or the text-mining techniques (see DavConsulting, 2023; Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland (MFA), 2023a).

paragraphs: The case study approach aimed to answer what the key management considerations for evaluation managers in public administration are and how they can overcome any barriers to engaging with new technologies. Case studies, producing comprehensive in-depth analyses, are well suited for analysing phenomena in their natural contexts (Priya, 2021;Swanborn, 2010). Using a real-life exploratory pilot, the case selection was purposive, drawing lessons for future assignments. Managing evaluation processes in a public office, the author was closely able to observe the evaluation process and outputs. The findings were analysed against standard evaluation management processes (Bamberger and Mabry, 2020;MFA, 2022) and findings from the literature.

Analysing a single case, the limitations to generalisability are obvious. Nevertheless, this article can benefit similar public organisations with limited resources. A careful description of the case, context and the process is needed (Priya, 2021).

sections.paper_id: Heinonen - 2025 - Piloting text-mining in a mixed-method evaluation on human rights Management propositions for small
paragraphs: The Evaluation of Human Rights and Human Rights-Based Approach (HRBA) in Finland's Development Policy and Cooperation by the Development Evaluation Unit of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs of Finland (MFA) piloted text-mining in 2022-2023. With three officers and a director, the unit commissions strategic centralised evaluations of complex evaluands such as policies, sectors or cooperation instruments (MFA, 2023b). The evaluations implemented by independent teams of external evaluation experts are overseen by the evaluation managers. Their role includes consultative planning, designing the terms of reference, steering the process, quality assurance, feedback and approval of deliverables, communication, dissemination and facilitating a management response (MFA, 2022).

Finland's unique HRBA includes a typology for the levels of HRBA application, 'the levels of ambition': human rights blind, sensitive, progressive and transformative. The minimum requirement is that all interventions 1 are HRBA-sensitive (MFA, 2015).

The evaluation examined how HRBA has been applied across the various cooperation instruments (MFA, 2023a). The text-mining techniques were selected due to their advantages in systematically handling high volumes of information across interventions. The data science component of the evaluation served (1) to inform the finalisation of the inception report and sampling and (2) to provide one line of evidence. The evaluation used a realist approach combined with contribution analysis. A theory of change outlined the contributions of HRBA towards development results. Evaluation methods included content analysis of document review, key informant interviews, an e-survey and context and issue-based case studies.

sections.paper_id: Heinonen - 2025 - Piloting text-mining in a mixed-method evaluation on human rights Management propositions for small
paragraphs: For insights into HRBA application, text-mining was applied to selected documents. Two most commonly available document types, project or programme plans and the latest reports, were analysed, representing both the design and implementation phases of the interventions.
The assumption was that evidence of HRBA application would be present in both types of documents.

The analysis was grounded in the HRBA Guidance Note (MFA, 2015). Yet, it was acknowledged that different approaches and ambitions for advancing human rights exist, which supported the realist approach. The evaluation used other methods for analysing the rationale and challenges of the cooperation instruments in implementing HRBA.

sections.paper_id: Heinonen - 2025 - Piloting text-mining in a mixed-method evaluation on human rights Management propositions for small
paragraphs: Text-mining was employed on a corpus of 840 documents -420 plans and 420 narrative reports. Document structures and formats varied from online fill-in PDF templates to Word or PDF documents, lacking a standardised information structure. Documents originated from diverse organisations, representing development cooperation across 60 + countries and regions. The data comprised textual and numerical evidence extracted through the selected text-mining techniques.

A rules-based technique was the natural starting point, with the HRBA typology and descriptors. The second method, machine learning and transformer models, responded to the limitations of the first and allowed more nuanced analysis beyond the predefined textual labels. This predictive analysis used text extracts to assess similarities against the levels of HRBA ambition. The third method allowed natural patterns to emerge from the corpus.

sections.paper_id: Heinonen - 2025 - Piloting text-mining in a mixed-method evaluation on human rights Management propositions for small
sections.header_norm: process and outputs of text mining
paragraphs: For the rules-based analysis, the HRBA typology was translated into textual labels representing the levels of ambition and their specifications. 2 The documents being in English and Finnish, labels were determined for both languages. For example, advocacy is a characteristic of the transformative level. The textual label of 'advoc*' was selected to indicate the transformative category.

The terms of reference were finalised iteratively. The analytical framework underwent six rounds of robustness checks and fine-tuning. The evaluation manager led the iterative process, producing multiple test versions and liaising with the evaluation team and colleagues. The final output was an Excel spreadsheet containing data on every intervention on their estimated HRBA levels.

Machine learning with transformer models used paragraphs of text from the corpus representative of the levels of HRBA ambition. Approximately ten text excerpts per ambition level were selected, requiring subject matter knowledge by the evaluation team and manager. Paragraphs from both plans and reports were selected. Using pre-trained language models, the similarity between the corpus and selected paragraphs was estimated. Only the English corpus (n = 624) was used due to the inadequate number of Finnish text extracts available. The output, an additional column in the spreadsheet, displayed the estimated HRBA levels based on this technique.

Topic modelling examined alternative language use and the co-occurrence of nouns with 'human rights'. It analysed, among others, interventions found below the minimum HRBA standard by the previous methods. The final outputs were lists of nouns, their frequencies and visualisations as word clouds.

Meta-data from the Ministry's management information system provided intervention details like geographical location, sector and self-assessed HRBA levels ('HRBA-markers'). These allowed comparisons with text-mining results. The self-assessed levels also served as benchmarks in calibrating the analytical framework.

sections.preview_tail: Meta-data from the Ministry's management information system provided intervention details like geographical location, sector and self-assessed HRBA levels ('HRBA-markers'). These allowed comparisons with text-mining results. The self-assessed levels also served as benchmarks in calibrating the analytical framework.

paragraphs: Obstacle 1: Limited in-house capability. One key hindrance to incorporating AI technologies within public administration is the lack of AI capability, with limited infrastructure and outdated technology (Desouza, 2018;Gil-García and Pardo, 2005;Hossin et al., 2023;Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). Although Finland is among the world's pioneers, digital transformation requires building organisational capabilities in addition to technical readiness (Rönkkö and Herneoja, 2021). There is a lack of technical personnel to introduce new technologies in public organisations (Gil-García and Pardo, 2005;Neumann et al., 2022). As the technologies are evolving rapidly, integrating them can be a challenge as well as keeping up with the latest developments (Liu, 2022). In the pilot, broader in-house readiness for data science methods did not yet exist. None of the staff being data scientists, the unit trained itself on the topic with customised workshops. In addition to understanding the basics of the various techniques, Van Noordt and Tangi (2023) note that managers need to understand trade-offs and the additional work needed for implementing any projects. These aspects were included in the tailored trainings.

With limited internal capabilities, it was vital for the unit to have partners with a range of expertise. This is not surprising as public administrations often depend on external partners for such infrastructure or services (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). Yet, many evaluators may not be familiar with these techniques, although new applications in evaluator education are emerging (Tilton et al., 2023). Evaluators must understand how these technologies work before using them and the skills may not yet be there (Head et al., 2023;Liu, 2022). In this case, the team leader was also trained in the basics of the techniques and their benefits.

Obstacle 2: Limited resources. Public administrations often struggle with time and resources, with little available for exploring new technologies (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). It can become burdensome for the public administration field to learn methods such as text-mining (Mao and Li, 2022). Hiring external experts can also be costly, particularly if the need is for a large team (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023) or applying DSAI in small-budget evaluations (Nielsen, 2023). The evaluation manager benchmarked cost estimates based on prior assignments and experiences from other donors and considered the relative weight to be given to the data science component. Being only a part of the overall evaluation, there was no need for a large team of data scientists: two experts, with one conducting most of the assignment. Costed options in the budget enabled adaptability and gradual progression.

The assignment spanned 8 months, with the evaluation manager dedicating over a 100 hours, about 16 workdays, to various tasks such as document collection, process management, crosschecks, feedback and organising public events. This ran parallel to managing the overall evaluation. This required the manager to set aside ample time for this assignment every week. The limited resources and in-house capabilities were the reasons for opting for outsourcing.

Obstacle 3: Procurement procedures. In the public sector, procurement processes can sometimes be stagnant and outdated, with complicated sets of rules and rigid frameworks applied (Desouza, 2018;Rönkkö and Herneoja, 2021). The existence of a framework agreement for evaluations facilitated overcoming this obstacle. Text-mining was employed as part of the overall evaluation design, and a separate data science component was procured. The service provider, responsible for proposing evaluation teams, was tasked to identify a partner for the data science component matching the requirements outlined in the terms of reference.

Engaging with just any data scientist is not advisable. Contextual knowledge is also required from data scientists (Engström and Norén, 2021). As in the case evaluation, the best results can be obtained by service providers that are familiar with the field. Using external providers for a one-off assignment avoids questions about how to manage dependencies created by maintaining such services (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). For organisations experimenting with DSAI, starting with a single evaluation assignment may provide an entry point even if no capabilities for scaling up exist.

Obstacle 4: Risk aversion. An environment for exploration may not exist in public offices (Mergel, 2018;Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). A culture of playing it safe can hinder IT projects without sufficient capabilities built first (Desouza, 2018;Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). Risks for public organisations typically include security risks, privacy concerns, regulation and legality concerns and intellectual property risks (Gil-García and Pardo, 2005;Head et al., 2023;Hossin et al., 2023;Mergel, 2018;Neumann et al., 2022;Patel, 2023;Reutter, 2022). Some public sector entities, such as independent innovation units, may have more freedom to experiment (Plantinga, 2024). Similarly, independent evaluation entities might enjoy a slightly higher degree of independence to take on pilots. Information security and system constraints required labour-intensive document handling by the manager, including document search against the selection criteria, saving in the right format and making available to the data scientists, bound by non-disclosure agreements. Experimenting with public information is an entry point. Lack of internal technical capability can add to risks in the tender preparation (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). These challenges were tackled by requesting information on risks and their management in proposals, including data security and safety considerations. Risks were assessed and discussed with the service provider. Another mitigating factor was designing an iterative process that allowed adaptation to any emergent concerns.

The risks for the overall evaluation included delays to the evaluation process and the uncertainty at the outset regarding the adaptability of methods to the complexities of the evaluation. To manage risks, the evaluation design was kept flexible, allowing the overall evaluation to progress in case of delays in the data science component.

Obstacle 5: Ethical considerations. Many discuss the ethical concerns of DSAI, particularly safety, transparency, accountability, inclusivity and equity (European Commission, 2019;Raftree, 2021;Reid, 2023;UNDG, 2023;UNDP, 2023;York and Bamberger, 2020). Governments and other organisations are only starting to understand the ethical implications of using data science (Bamberger and Mabry, 2020). Higher standards are required from public agencies. They must consider ethical and social matters, safeguard transparency and fairness, and pay attention to any biases, prejudices, non-discrimination and harm (Desouza, 2018;Head et al., 2023;Neumann et al., 2022;Reid, 2023). This entails going beyond the mere generation of credible data (Abrahams et al., 2021).

Ethical considerations were part of the training content for the evaluation unit. The service provider was requested to highlight any ethical concerns in their proposal. Ethical issues were regularly discussed among the data scientists, evaluation manager and the evaluation team. Solutions included using paragraphs, not entire documents, to minimise any biases related to organisation types or cooperation channels. Using different levels of fluency in the text extracts reduced biases towards language proficiency. Sensitive information was omitted from final reports, and data aggregated beyond individual organisations.

In theory, using text-mining across the full sampling frame can reduce the risks of nonrepresentative data due to a sampling bias. Using the best available language models reduced biases. Yet, the Finnish language models might not have been as developed as the English ones. The extent that the documents were representative of the end beneficiaries was beyond the control of the assignment.

sections.paper_id: Heinonen - 2025 - Piloting text-mining in a mixed-method evaluation on human rights Management propositions for small
paragraphs: Obstacle 6: Relation to the evaluation assignment. Finding relevance and alignment between organisational goals and technology use can be a challenge (Dawes, 2009;Gil-García and Pardo, 2005). Managers are needed to align such endeavours with the organisational goals and tasks (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). Based on the understanding of the techniques, a decision must be taken on what the objectives for the use of the data science techniques are. Embedded into the overall evaluation process and its phases, the separate terms of reference for the data science component were nested in those for the overall evaluation.

Evaluations commissioned by governments are expected to not only serve accountability but also to facilitate institutional learning and use (Levelt and Pouw, 2022). The decision to use text-mining methods was based on, first, whether they could generate evidence for the evaluation questions and, second, whether they would have any added value in generating stand-alone results that would facilitate learning. In the case evaluation, the techniques informed the
Table 1. Management propositions, preparation phase.

 Identify internal capabilities and capacities at the outset.  Check organisation's policies and rules, particularly on data and information security.  Invest in training and skills development in-house as well as for evaluation teams.  Consider outsourcing to manage limited resources. A one-off pilot reduces long-term dependencies.  Leverage existing frameworks or agreements to simplify procurement.  Require openness to new methods from the evaluation team.  Design an iterative process that allows adaptation to emerging concerns and/or exploratory refinement of evaluation questions or analytical frameworks.  Request information on risks and ethical issues in tender proposals. Incorporate them into training content and early discussions.  Budget with costed options to allow adaptability and gradual progression.
Source: Author's analysis.
inception phase and the overall analysis, running alongside the evaluation. A modular approach allowed the data science component to proceed at its own pace.

It is important to facilitate the understanding by domain experts during the process. Co-creation can be a way to ensure such expertise (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). Co-creation was employed during the finalisation of the terms of reference with domain and reference group members. The iterative optimisation of the analytical framework was finalised jointly, the evaluation team factoring in domain expertise on human rights.

Obstacle 7: Understanding the nature of evidence. Using data science methods may sometimes be portrayed as highly rigorous and objective, producing false promises of objectivity and accuracy. These techniques entail multiple points of human selection, many assumptions are used in data processing and the final interpretation is subjective (Head et al., 2023;Hossin et al., 2023).

Designing the case evaluation required an understanding of the nature of evidence by textmining. Despite quantitative outputs, it was evident that the nature of evidence was predominantly qualitative, stemming from the nature of textual data. The analytical framework and text extract selection involved qualitative judgements.

Evaluations are not fully neutral or objective (Weiss, 2013). They are also political and value-laden, and evaluators have unique positions (Levelt and Pouw, 2022;Preskill, 2013). From that perspective, the selection of textual paragraphs based on subject matter expertise does not differ from the more traditional types of qualitative content analyses.

Because the purpose was not to establish a fine-tuned analytical model to capture precise HRBA levels, the outputs from these three techniques were neither compared against each other nor was convergence sought. The corpora were not comparable. Understanding that there cannot be one clear-cut truth in the documents, the aim was a descriptive rich picture of the nature of the evaluand. Should the aim have been an accurate predictive model, more rounds of fine-tuning and tests for rigorousness would have been needed. That approach would have served a different purpose and applied a different set of assumptions about the nature of the evaluand. For a one-off evaluation on a highly subjective topic, it is not very meaningful.

Obstacle 8: A plethora of unstructured, incomplete information. The challenges of using new technologies include data availability, incomplete coverage, non-representative or non-inclusive data, data quality and errors (Garcia, 2021;Raftree, 2021;York and Bamberger, 2020). In public administration, information is often produced by various entities across the field of governance. The heterogeneity of sources and differing formats can cause low interoperability (Rönkkö and Herneoja, 2021). What data is available and of what quality is typically unclear to public officials (Reutter, 2022).

The time needed to get to know the source data can become one of the obstacles (Reutter, 2022). Regarding the case, the evaluation manager was familiar with the type of corpus involved, having used the same documentation in other evaluations. The evaluation manager reviewed the foreseen corpus. The types of documents, formats and their characteristics were described in the terms of reference and discussed with the service providers. No unified information structure existed; however, this proved not to be a major obstacle.

While collecting the documents, the manager had to decide which ones to exclude due to insufficient quality. Some were disqualified due to missing information. The remaining set was considered representative enough, covering all interventions of the temporal scope of the evaluation. The inherent errors in data found during the iteration phase were cross-checked and either the document was excluded, the analytical framework adjusted or meta-data corrected. The benefits of the outsourced approach were that the data scientists responsible for coding and running the analyses also conducted data cleaning and parsing.

Obstacle 9: The complexity challenge. Evaluations increasingly address complex projects or programmes (Bamberger and Mabry, 2020). The pilot was putting the text-mining techniques to a real test regarding their ability to handle complexities and multiple layers of analytical elements. The topic at hand, the evaluation design and the analytical framework were highly complex.

The topic of HRBA is not a straightforward one, with no one agreed definition. Although based on international standards and approaches, Finland has a unique approach. Each level of ambition included several substantive issues, which affected the number of textual labels (27). As the seven cooperation instruments used different document formats, an analysis at the overall organisational level was not meaningful. Two document types, project plans and reports, were identified as the common denominators for all interventions. Language (English and Finnish) created another aspect of complexity. This complexity called for careful identification of units and levels of analysis. These were reflected in the analytic framework, in structuring the corpus and in selecting outputs for final reporting.

Obstacle 10: Limited conclusions. Usability can be an obstacle for public offices (Gil-García and Pardo, 2005). It was important for the manager to understand what conclusions could be drawn from the evidence. No direct conclusions were possible based on these methods only, as the actual implementation of HRBA would need to be verified beyond documents. The techniques could reveal how HRBA is evidenced in project or programme documentation, but not why certain interventions used specific language.

No one method can address complex evaluands. Mixed-methods can strengthen evaluation design, yield more relevant and useful knowledge and address complexity. They can expose unexpected results, provide a deeper understanding of the evaluand or the context and generate a greater variety of perspectives, nuances or divergence. Design options include concurrent, sequential, and multilevel analysis and synthesis (Bamberger and Mabry, 2020;USAID, 2010). A mixed-method design prevented making hasty conclusions based on text-mining alone. Qualitative methods, including interviews and traditional document review, addressed the blind spots overlooked by the data science component. The sequential analysis within the data science component suited well the interactive nature of the process. It facilitated learning by experimentation and informed the sampling. At the overall evaluation level, concurrent analysis of other data sources during both the inception and data collection phase was a strategy to manage time, risks and resource constraints. The multilevel analysis addressed the complexity elements. An overarching synthesis was made based on the evaluation team's sense-making of the various lines of evidence in line with the evaluation matrix.

Obstacle 6: Relation to the evaluation assignment. Finding relevance and alignment between organisational goals and technology use can be a challenge (Dawes, 2009;Gil-García and Pardo, 2005). Managers are needed to align such endeavours with the organisational goals and tasks (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). Based on the understanding of the techniques, a decision must be taken on what the objectives for the use of the data science techniques are. Embedded into the overall evaluation process and its phases, the separate terms of reference for the data science component were nested in those for the overall evaluation.

Evaluations commissioned by governments are expected to not only serve accountability but also to facilitate institutional learning and use (Levelt and Pouw, 2022). The decision to use text-mining methods was based on, first, whether they could generate evidence for the evaluation questions and, second, whether they would have any added value in generating stand-alone results that would facilitate learning. In the case evaluation, the techniques informed the

Table 1. Management propositions, preparation phase.

 Identify internal capabilities and capacities at the outset.  Check organisation's policies and rules, particularly on data and information security.  Invest in training and skills development in-house as well as for evaluation teams.  Consider outsourcing to manage limited resources. A one-off pilot reduces long-term dependencies.  Leverage existing frameworks or agreements to simplify procurement.  Require openness to new methods from the evaluation team.  Design an iterative process that allows adaptation to emerging concerns and/or exploratory refinement of evaluation questions or analytical frameworks.  Request information on risks and ethical issues in tender proposals. Incorporate them into training content and early discussions.  Budget with costed options to allow adaptability and gradual progression.

Source: Author's analysis.

inception phase and the overall analysis, running alongside the evaluation. A modular approach allowed the data science component to proceed at its own pace.

It is important to facilitate the understanding by domain experts during the process. Co-creation can be a way to ensure such expertise (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). Co-creation was employed during the finalisation of the terms of reference with domain and reference group members. The iterative optimisation of the analytical framework was finalised jointly, the evaluation team factoring in domain expertise on human rights.

Obstacle 7: Understanding the nature of evidence. Using data science methods may sometimes be portrayed as highly rigorous and objective, producing false promises of objectivity and accuracy. These techniques entail multiple points of human selection, many assumptions are used in data processing and the final interpretation is subjective (Head et al., 2023;Hossin et al., 2023).

Designing the case evaluation required an understanding of the nature of evidence by textmining. Despite quantitative outputs, it was evident that the nature of evidence was predominantly qualitative, stemming from the nature of textual data. The analytical framework and text extract selection involved qualitative judgements.

Evaluations are not fully neutral or objective (Weiss, 2013). They are also political and value-laden, and evaluators have unique positions (Levelt and Pouw, 2022;Preskill, 2013). From that perspective, the selection of textual paragraphs based on subject matter expertise does not differ from the more traditional types of qualitative content analyses.

Because the purpose was not to establish a fine-tuned analytical model to capture precise HRBA levels, the outputs from these three techniques were neither compared against each other nor was convergence sought. The corpora were not comparable. Understanding that there cannot be one clear-cut truth in the documents, the aim was a descriptive rich picture of the nature of the evaluand. Should the aim have been an accurate predictive model, more rounds of fine-tuning and tests for rigorousness would have been needed. That approach would have served a different purpose and applied a different set of assumptions about the nature of the evaluand. For a one-off evaluation on a highly subjective topic, it is not very meaningful.

Obstacle 8: A plethora of unstructured, incomplete information. The challenges of using new technologies include data availability, incomplete coverage, non-representative or non-inclusive data, data quality and errors (Garcia, 2021;Raftree, 2021;York and Bamberger, 2020). In public administration, information is often produced by various entities across the field of governance. The heterogeneity of sources and differing formats can cause low interoperability (Rönkkö and Herneoja, 2021). What data is available and of what quality is typically unclear to public officials (Reutter, 2022).

The time needed to get to know the source data can become one of the obstacles (Reutter, 2022). Regarding the case, the evaluation manager was familiar with the type of corpus involved, having used the same documentation in other evaluations. The evaluation manager reviewed the foreseen corpus. The types of documents, formats and their characteristics were described in the terms of reference and discussed with the service providers. No unified information structure existed; however, this proved not to be a major obstacle.

While collecting the documents, the manager had to decide which ones to exclude due to insufficient quality. Some were disqualified due to missing information. The remaining set was considered representative enough, covering all interventions of the temporal scope of the evaluation. The inherent errors in data found during the iteration phase were cross-checked and either the document was excluded, the analytical framework adjusted or meta-data corrected. The benefits of the outsourced approach were that the data scientists responsible for coding and running the analyses also conducted data cleaning and parsing.

Obstacle 9: The complexity challenge. Evaluations increasingly address complex projects or programmes (Bamberger and Mabry, 2020). The pilot was putting the text-mining techniques to a real test regarding their ability to handle complexities and multiple layers of analytical elements. The topic at hand, the evaluation design and the analytical framework were highly complex.

The topic of HRBA is not a straightforward one, with no one agreed definition. Although based on international standards and approaches, Finland has a unique approach. Each level of ambition included several substantive issues, which affected the number of textual labels (27). As the seven cooperation instruments used different document formats, an analysis at the overall organisational level was not meaningful. Two document types, project plans and reports, were identified as the common denominators for all interventions. Language (English and Finnish) created another aspect of complexity. This complexity called for careful identification of units and levels of analysis. These were reflected in the analytic framework, in structuring the corpus and in selecting outputs for final reporting.

Obstacle 10: Limited conclusions. Usability can be an obstacle for public offices (Gil-García and Pardo, 2005). It was important for the manager to understand what conclusions could be drawn from the evidence. No direct conclusions were possible based on these methods only, as the actual implementation of HRBA would need to be verified beyond documents. The techniques could reveal how HRBA is evidenced in project or programme documentation, but not why certain interventions used specific language.

No one method can address complex evaluands. Mixed-methods can strengthen evaluation design, yield more relevant and useful knowledge and address complexity. They can expose unexpected results, provide a deeper understanding of the evaluand or the context and generate a greater variety of perspectives, nuances or divergence. Design options include concurrent, sequential, and multilevel analysis and synthesis (Bamberger and Mabry, 2020;USAID, 2010). A mixed-method design prevented making hasty conclusions based on text-mining alone. Qualitative methods, including interviews and traditional document review, addressed the blind spots overlooked by the data science component. The sequential analysis within the data science component suited well the interactive nature of the process. It facilitated learning by experimentation and informed the sampling. At the overall evaluation level, concurrent analysis of other data sources during both the inception and data collection phase was a strategy to manage time, risks and resource constraints. The multilevel analysis addressed the complexity elements. An overarching synthesis was made based on the evaluation team's sense-making of the various lines of evidence in line with the evaluation matrix.

Text-mining added value cutting across the entire intervention portfolio, allowing the emergence of topics, terminology and patterns otherwise likely to have been missed, and providing a big picture. The complementing lines of evidence enabled a more contextualised analysis, exploring the reasons behind the various levels of HRBA application and evaluating policy implementation beyond documents.

Management reflections. Planning the data science component involved a balance between the time and effort spent on it versus the overall evaluation. The elements of complexity certainly affected the management process, requiring detailed attention to each aspect. To simplify, the evaluation could have only used English documents, although that would have excluded some cooperation instruments. A more straightforward conceptual topic with clearer definitions would have reduced complexity. An analysis of 'human right*' would have been the most simplified approach. For organisations piloting these techniques, starting with something less complex might be an entry point. The type of mixed-method design depends on whether the data is intended to inform the evaluation design during the inception phase or serve as a line of evidence within the overall body of evidence. For the former, a sequential approach could be ideal, and for the latter, a concurrent analysis could become more cost-effective.

sections.paper_id: Heinonen - 2025 - Piloting text-mining in a mixed-method evaluation on human rights Management propositions for small
paragraphs: Obstacle 11: Management structures and processes. Lack of organisational structures and processes in public organisations during the early stages of adopting new technologies can be a barrier (Mergel, 2018). They can also be siloed and static (Rönkkö and Herneoja, 2021). With no pre-existing avenues for piloting, the outsourced data science component needed to be integrated into the existing evaluation management structures and processes. An ideal configuration for management is a small core team working with externals, and externalising as much technical capability as possible (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). Issues were discussed
Table 3. Management propositions, design phase.

 Ensure the evidence could meaningfully inform the evaluation questions.  Set DSAI objectives based on their alignment with evaluation objectives and learning potential.  Review and describe source data characteristics and limitations carefully.  Employ mixed-methods, describing complementarity and triangulation expected, and the rationale for DSAI techniques.  Define the level of detail of DSAI analysis. Consider a separate annex or a report for detailed methodspecific analysis.  Ensure evaluation teams and end-users understand the nature of conclusions, their limitations and any inherent subjectivity in data processing and interpretation.  Leverage the expertise of data scientists to handle data cleaning and parsing effectively.

among the small evaluation management team, consisting of the evaluation manager, the team leader and the service coordinator for the framework arrangement. To facilitate understanding between domain experts and data scientists (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023), the regular evaluation management processes involved the reference group and the evaluation team. The manager also had to consider how to address the human element and any potential resistance (Dawes, 2009;Garcia, 2021;Gil-García and Pardo, 2005). Compared with other evaluations, the process entailed more frequent communication on the rationale for the selected methods.

Uncertainty about innovation outcomes can be a barrier (Mergel, 2018). To address the uncertainties of the iterative process and to ensure utility to the overall evaluation, two solutions were found critical. First, with a modular approach and implementation parallel to the inception phase, generating separate outputs at different stages. Second, leveraging the evaluation management team to ensure relevance to the evaluation assignment, channelling substantive inputs, running numerous cross-checks and implementing quality assurance measures.

Obstacle 12: Quality assurance. With text-mining methods, often called 'black box' methods, there is uncertainty about how algorithms generate outputs (Bamberger and Mabry, 2020). This poses transparency challenges for evaluation. On the contrary, text-mining is feasible and can be reliable and effective (Mao and Li, 2022). Integrating the data science component into the evaluation management ensured that structures were in place to assess the credibility, accuracy and reliability of its outputs.

Credibility was increased by employing mixed-methods to answer the evaluation questions. The data science component used multiple techniques rather than a single technique. A clear linkage with corpus documents was maintained through identification codes, verifiable by the evaluation manager or team members. The appropriateness of the technology being one of the defining factors (Nielsen, 2023), frequent discussions with the service provider assessed the suitability of each text-mining technique.

Accuracy improved through an iterative approach in designing the evaluation terms of reference and finalising the analytical framework. The evaluation manager and team flagged potential errors. Six rounds of cross-checks, pilot tests and calibration were conducted, to arrive at a version that would perform at a satisfactory level within the limitations of the assignment. Knowledge of the subject matter played a key role.

Reliability was managed by applying a systematic set of document selection criteria per instrument, using state-of-the-art pre-trained machine learning models and documenting the process in detail. Faced with variations in templates and language proficiency, this approach enabled some generalisability at the cooperation instrument level. To counter the 'black box' aspect, transparency was improved via detailed and open reporting, including the text extracts and limitations.

Obstacle 13: The audience not understanding the results. A data-skills gap exists in the public sector, particularly in data science literacy (Overton and Kleinschmit, 2022). Converting new knowledge into action is another challenge (Rönkkö and Herneoja, 2021). The role of a 'translator' is important in understanding policy needs and context, being the junction between data scientists and policymakers and ensuring that outputs are delivered in an understandable format (Arnaboldi and Azzone, 2020;Davenport, 2018;UN Global Pulse, 2016). The evaluation manager and team leader provided feedback on the reporting outputs. The manager's knowledge of the audience guided reporting requirements. The manager also translated findings to end-users in plain language and explained the limitations. The manager produced and disseminated messages, briefs and summaries that explained the results in less technical terms.

Management reflections. Process-related improvements could have included even closer alignment with the overall evaluation management process, especially during the inception phase. Spending more time to validate the labels and the text extracts could have improved the quality further. However, balancing the data science pilot with the overall evaluation assignment was necessary. A pre-determined weight helped draw a line to perfecting the data science analysis. With additional time and resources, further exploration of topic modelling could have included the nature of language in the various cooperation instruments.

The manager produced and disseminated messages, briefs and summaries that explained the results in less technical terms.

Management reflections. Process-related improvements could have included even closer alignment with the overall evaluation management process, especially during the inception phase. Spending more time to validate the labels and the text extracts could have improved the quality further. However, balancing the data science pilot with the overall evaluation assignment was necessary. A pre-determined weight helped draw a line to perfecting the data science analysis. With additional time and resources, further exploration of topic modelling could have included the nature of language in the various cooperation instruments.

sections.paper_id: Heinonen - 2025 - Piloting text-mining in a mixed-method evaluation on human rights Management propositions for small
paragraphs: While evaluation must serve political, policy and management decision-making, it should also be seen as a public good (Oksanen, 2018). Text-mining techniques, such as the ones used in the pilot case, can generate public value in systematically assessing the whole field of governance, previously considered the sampling frame. Evaluations act as vehicles for generating public information and serving accountability needs. Civil servants must find new ways of working with these technologies (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). Pilots such as this can pave the way to such a transformation. The public sector evaluation commissioners can build demand and contribute to building capabilities. Whereas technical expertise may be acquired through outsourcing, internal leadership cannot (Van Noordt and Tangi, 2023). The evaluation manager is crucial in taking up new methods within an organisation. They channel knowledge and ensure alignment with organisational objectives and context. They can serve as a sounding board to interpret results or effectively 'translate' them for end-users, especially when data scientists may not be familiar with all the specificities of a particular policy approach or evaluand.

Techniques such as text-mining can offer new efficient ways to analyse data. They can add value by offering quantified data, particularly for evaluations reliant on interviews and document reviews. There is high potential in text-based analyses on conceptual topics such as the HRBA. In this case, text-mining served well in providing descriptive data. The text-mining methods generated valuable information for the overall evaluation and provided a systematic overview. The analysis indicates how various instruments and partners understand HRBA. The results prompt considerations for improving project application formats, funding assessment criteria
Table 4. Management propositions, implementation and reporting phase.

 Integrate the assignment into the existing evaluation management structures, with roles and responsibilities clearly described.  Conduct frequent discussions with service providers to assess the quality and suitability of the techniques.  Improve transparency by detailing the methodologies and decision-making behind data selection and analysis.  Facilitate understanding between domain experts and data scientists through regular evaluation management processes.  Tailor reporting requirements and communication products by the audiences.  Plan how to 'translate' to end-users to ensure the utility of results. and reporting requirements. The results can inform the revision of the Guidance Note, and identification of capacity or training needs.

Being a pilot, some of the new management considerations are related to finding ways of using the organisational structures and capacities available, others to maintaining linkages with the overall evaluation process. The iterative process was inevitable due to an analytical framework that needed testing and calibration before the analysis could be performed. Similarly, it was not entirely certain which text-mining techniques would work best with the type of corpus used. Outsourcing enabled more flexibility. The modular approach enabled learning from earlier stages. Compared with the more typical evaluation management process, the workload was significantly front-loaded as anticipated, involving tasks such as document search and numerous rounds of iteration and cross-checks. This also influenced quality assurance measures, making them more process-oriented. Evaluation managers must carefully consider the capacities and availability of resources. They should be prepared to engage in hands-on management and cross-checks. Although this article has emphasised and focused on the role of the evaluation manager, the roles of the evaluation team, service coordinator, consultancy firms and data scientists are by no means less significant.

Text-mining is not an exact science. In this regard, it does not differ from several other evaluation methods. Evaluators need to make analytical choices and decisions when employing any method, exercising their judgement throughout. At best, text-mining can provide estimates, but when combined with other methods, the evaluation team can triangulate findings and provide a comprehensive assessment of the merits and worth of the evaluand.

As in the pilot, new technologies cannot fully replace evaluators or evaluation managers. The need for a deep understanding of the subject matter, the contextual knowledge and research design and process remains (Klami et al., 2022;Nielsen, 2023). Text-mining alone would not have provided straightforward answers to evaluation questions on policy implementation. This challenge is amplified by the complex nature of the evaluand, which lacks a single, definitive measure. Yet, the methods seem to have passed the complexity challenge. The inherent limitations of the techniques call for a mixed-method approach, requiring evaluative judgement from professional evaluators.

For public administrations, engaging with new technologies can seem like an obstacle course. Literature has highlighted many challenges and risks, and rightfully so. This article demonstrated the feasibility of piloting new technologies such as text-mining in evaluations by public administrations with limited human resources. Using these methods can be resourceintensive. Careful consideration of available capacities is imperative. With no in-house data scientists, effective guidance from the evaluation manager becomes even more critical. Best practices include familiarising oneself with the methods, providing training in advance, and early liaison with data scientists to sensitise them to the client's needs.

Mixed-method designs are well suited to function as an avenue for new technologies. Understanding the nature of the evidence generated, recognising the potential and discussing suitability are paramount. One does not have to become a data scientist; what is more crucial is a general understanding of the kind of information that can, and should, result from the process, and in a way that (a) is informative and of quality in its own right, (b) feeds into the evaluation process as a useful line of evidence. As technological tools continue to permeate various societal spheres, the evaluation community is well placed to embrace the opportunities through mixed-method designs -and the challenges are a matter of management.
